title: Types of Discontinuities by Product Form
narrationScript: Every part walks into your booth carrying its biography. The way it was made decides which discontinuities it can contain, where they sit, and which way they run. That is why a Level Two inspector studies product forms. If you know how the part was made, you know what to hunt for and which way to magnetize before you ever flip the current on. Start at the beginning of almost every steel part, the ingot. Molten steel poured into a mold shrinks as it freezes, and the last metal to solidify, up at the top center, runs short of feed metal. The result is a shrinkage cavity called pipe. The melt also traps gas, which freezes in as blowholes and porosity, and it carries nonmetallic material, slag, refractory fragments, and deoxidation products, that freeze in as inclusions. Alloying elements can also distribute unevenly as the ingot freezes, which we call segregation. The top of the ingot is cropped off to remove most of the pipe, but anything that survives gets passed downstream. And that matters, because an ingot is rarely the finished product. It is rolled, forged, drawn, or extruded into wrought shapes, and every surviving discontinuity is worked right along with the steel. Pipe and inclusions stretch into long thin stringers. A blowhole or a crack at the ingot surface gets rolled flat and closed on the outside only, becoming a seam, a very tight, very straight, longitudinal discontinuity at the surface of bar stock. Flattened inclusions and porosity in plate become laminations, wide thin separations lying parallel to the surface. Forging adds its own problems. If metal folds over itself and fails to fuse, you get a lap, a smooth discontinuity lying at a shallow angle to the surface, which holds particles weakly. If the center of a section is worked too cold or reduced too fast, internal ruptures called bursts tear open. Bars that cool unevenly after hot rolling can split lengthwise in cooling cracks, which run roughly longitudinal but wander, and often go deeper than seams. Castings skip the wrought chain because they freeze directly into shape, so they keep their solidification defects. Shrinkage gathers at section changes and hot spots that froze last, and shows as a broad fuzzy patch when it sits near the surface. Gas porosity scatters as rounded cavities. If two streams of metal meet after their surfaces have skinned over and fail to fuse, the result is a cold shut, a smooth curved line on the surface. If the casting is restrained while it is still hot and weak, it tears, and a hot tear shows as a ragged, branched crack, usually at a section change or a re-entrant corner. Sand and slag from the mold freeze in as inclusions at or near the surface. Welds are small castings made under the worst possible conditions, so they collect their own family. Incomplete penetration leaves an unfused root. Lack of fusion leaves unfused walls between weld passes or at the bevel face. Slag gets trapped between passes. Gas makes porosity. And cracking comes in many flavors, crater cracks where a pass was stopped abruptly, longitudinal and transverse cracks in the bead, toe cracks where the bead meets the base metal, and delayed hydrogen cracks hiding under the bead in the heat affected zone, sometimes forming hours or days after welding. Now connect all of this to magnetic particle testing with two rules. First, orientation. A discontinuity shows best when flux crosses it at a right angle, and acceptably down to about forty five degrees. Seams, stringers, and cooling cracks run along the bar, so circular magnetization, current through the part, finds them. Transverse cracks in a shaft need longitudinal magnetization from a coil. Since real parts can hide flaws in any direction, full coverage always means two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other. Second, appearance. A tight surface crack pulls strong leakage flux and builds a sharp, dense, tightly held particle line. The deeper a discontinuity sits below the surface, the weaker and more spread out the leakage, so subsurface flaws like bursts, deep inclusions, and underbead cracks show as broad fuzzy indications, when they show at all. A smooth lap or cold shut leaks weakly and builds a faint line that is easy to wipe away, so handle the part gently and look twice. Learn the families, ingot, casting, wrought, and weld, and indications stop surprising you. You predicted them before the shot.
imagePrompt: Under deep violet ultraviolet light in a darkened inspection booth, a technician rotates a forged steel connecting rod as a sharp green-yellow fluorescent crack indication glows along its flash line.

sections.heading: The Part's Biography Predicts Its Flaws
sections.body:
Discontinuities are not random. Each manufacturing process leaves its own fingerprints, and every process step inherits whatever the previous one left behind. An ingot's shrinkage cavity becomes a forging's internal burst; a blowhole at an ingot surface becomes a seam in bar stock. Knowing the production route tells you three things before you energize a circuit:

- **What to expect** — the discontinuity types that process can create.
- **Where it lives** — surface or subsurface, and at which features (section changes, weld toes, flash lines, fillets).
- **Which way it runs** — and therefore which field direction you need, because leakage is strongest when flux crosses the discontinuity at 45–90°.

That last point is the working payoff. Seams run longitudinally in rolled bar, so circular magnetization (a head shot or central conductor) crosses them. Fatigue cracks in shafts and many weld cracks run transverse to the axis, so a coil shot or a properly oriented yoke is the answer. Full coverage of unknown orientations always takes two roughly perpendicular magnetizations — typical practice in documents such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V, Article 7.

sections.heading: Ingots: Where It All Starts
sections.body:
Nearly every wrought steel product begins as an ingot or continuously cast bloom, and the freezing of that first shape writes the opening chapter.

- **Pipe** — molten steel shrinks as it solidifies; the last liquid, at the top center, runs out of feed metal and leaves a shrinkage cavity. The cropped top removes most of it, but surviving pipe travels downstream as an internal separation.
- **Blowholes and porosity** — dissolved gases come out of solution during freezing and are trapped as rounded cavities, near the surface or deep in the section.
- **Nonmetallic inclusions** — slag, refractory fragments, and deoxidation products frozen into the metal. Later working stretches them into stringers.
- **Segregation** — uneven distribution of alloying elements and impurities between the first and last metal to freeze, which can locally alter mechanical properties and even magnetic permeability.

Ingot discontinuities matter to you mostly because of what they become: rolled, forged, and drawn material carries them forward in elongated, flattened form, aligned with the direction of working.

sections.caption: How ingot discontinuities travel into wrought products: pipe and inclusions elongate into internal separations and stringers, surface blowholes roll flat into seams, and in plate they spread into laminations.

sections.heading: Castings: Frozen-In Problems
sections.body:
A casting freezes directly into its final shape, so everything that happens during solidification stays in the part.

- **Shrinkage** — cavities or sponge-like porosity where heavy sections and hot spots froze last without feed metal; expect it at section changes and fillet junctions. Near-surface shrinkage gives a broad, fuzzy MT patch.
- **Gas porosity and blowholes** — rounded cavities from trapped or evolved gas, scattered or clustered, producing weak rounded indications when near the surface.
- **Cold shut** — two metal streams met after their surfaces had skinned over and failed to fuse. It appears as a smooth, curved, crack-like surface line, often weakly held because the walls are smooth and oxide-coated.
- **Hot tears** — the casting tore while hot and weak because the mold or a heavy section restrained its contraction. Expect ragged, branched indications at section changes and re-entrant corners.
- **Sand and slag inclusions** — mold material or dross frozen in at or near the surface.

Note the diagnostic pairing: **cold shut = smooth and curved; hot tear = ragged and branched**. Shape plus location is often enough to name the flaw.

sections.heading: Wrought Sections and Parts
sections.body:
Rolling, forging, drawing, and extrusion reshape the ingot — and its discontinuities.

- **Seams** — ingot or billet surface blowholes and cracks rolled into tight, straight, longitudinal surface discontinuities in bar and tubing. Very tight, sometimes intermittent; the classic circular-magnetization target.
- **Laminations** — inclusions, pipe, or porosity flattened and spread parallel to the surfaces of rolled plate. Because they lie parallel to both the surface and the usual flux path, MT on the plate face will not reliably show them; they appear at cut edges and machined bores.
- **Stringers** — inclusions elongated in the working direction, usually subsurface; expect weak, fuzzy, longitudinal indications when they approach the surface.
- **Forging laps** — metal folded over and squeezed but not fused, lying at a shallow angle to the surface. Smooth oxide-coated walls make faint, easily wiped indications.
- **Forging bursts** — internal or surface ruptures from working the metal too cold or reducing it too fast; internal bursts show broad and fuzzy under HWDC or FWDC, if at all.
- **Cooling cracks** — longitudinal cracks from uneven cooling of bar after rolling; they wander slightly off-axis and typically run deeper than seams.

Wrought flaw orientation follows the grain — overwhelmingly longitudinal — which is why bar work leans so heavily on circular magnetization.

sections.caption: Common weld discontinuities in cross-section: toe crack, underbead cracking in the HAZ, lack of sidewall fusion, slag, porosity, undercut, and incomplete root penetration.

sections.heading: Welds: A Family of Their Own
sections.body:
A weld is a small casting poured into a moving mold, surrounded by a heat-affected zone (HAZ) that was quenched and stressed — so welds combine casting-type flaws with crack families all their own.

- **Incomplete penetration** — an unfused root; a straight, tight, linear target on the root side.
- **Lack of fusion** — sidewall or interpass surfaces that never melted together; usually subsurface, showing broad and fuzzy if it shows at all.
- **Slag inclusions** — trapped flux between passes; irregular and subsurface.
- **Porosity** — rounded, scattered, weakly held indications.
- **Undercut** — a groove melted into the toe; MT may show a weak line that visual examination confirms as geometry.
- **Cracks** — longitudinal, transverse, crater cracks in an abruptly stopped pool, toe cracks at the bead-to-base-metal junction, and **underbead (delayed hydrogen) cracks** in the HAZ. Underbead cracking is subsurface and may form hours or days after welding — one reason some codes impose a waiting period before final MT of susceptible steels — and it favors HWDC for its subsurface reach.

Toe and crater cracks give sharp, dense lines. Subsurface lack of fusion and underbead cracking give soft, fuzzy signatures that make weld MT a two-direction, current-type-conscious exercise.

sections.title: Two Rules That Sort Almost Everything
sections.body:
**Orientation rule:** leakage — and detectability — is strongest with flux 45–90° to the discontinuity. Choose circular magnetization for longitudinal flaws, longitudinal magnetization (coil or yoke) for transverse flaws, and use two roughly perpendicular magnetizations for full coverage.

**Appearance rule:** sharp, dense, tightly held = tight surface crack or seam. Broad, fuzzy, weakly held = subsurface — or a smooth-walled lap or cold shut. Handle gently and confirm before you wipe anything away.

sections.caption: Quick reference: who made the flaw, what MT shows, and the field that finds it.
rows: Rolled bar or tubing (ingot surface flaws)
Faint smooth line at flash line or section change, weakly held
Ragged, branched line at a section change
Smooth, curved, crack-like line, weakly held
Hidden on the plate face; lines at cut edges and bores
Field across the expected crack line

figures.title: From ingot to wrought: how discontinuities travel

interactive.title: Name That Discontinuity: Casting, Wrought, or Weld?
interactive.intro: Classify each discontinuity by the product form that creates it. Think about the process — solidification, mechanical working, or fusion — that each flaw requires.

items.label: Cold shut
items.why: Two metal streams skinned over before merging in the mold — a casting solidification flaw with a smooth, curved surface trace.

items.label: Hot tear
items.why: Hot, weak metal tore when the mold or a heavy section restrained contraction — ragged and branched, at section changes.

items.label: Shrinkage cavity at a thick-to-thin junction
items.why: Hot spots that freeze last without feed metal are a casting problem; near-surface shrinkage shows as a broad fuzzy patch.

items.label: Seam
items.why: An ingot surface blowhole or crack rolled flat into a tight, straight, longitudinal surface discontinuity in bar stock.

items.label: Forging lap
items.why: Metal folded over and squeezed without fusing during forging — smooth-walled, at a shallow angle to the surface.

items.label: Lamination
items.why: Flattened inclusions or porosity spread parallel to plate surfaces by rolling — visible only at edges and bores.

items.label: Crater crack
items.why: Shrinkage cracking in a weld pool that was stopped abruptly — a star-shaped or short linear crack at the end of a pass.

items.label: Lack of sidewall fusion
items.why: Weld metal that never fused to the bevel face — typically subsurface, showing broad and fuzzy when it shows.

items.label: Underbead crack
items.why: Delayed hydrogen cracking in the heat-affected zone beneath the bead — subsurface, sometimes forming days after welding.

checkQuestions.question: You are inspecting hot-rolled bar stock for seams. Which magnetization puts flux across them?
options: A coil shot — seams run transverse to the bar axis
Circular magnetization (head shot or central conductor) — seams run longitudinally, so circumferential flux crosses them at 90°
A yoke with its poles aligned along the bar axis
The residual method in any direction — orientation does not matter for seams
checkQuestions.explanation: Seams are tight longitudinal surface discontinuities; circular magnetization sends flux around the circumference, crossing a longitudinal flaw at roughly a right angle where leakage is strongest.

checkQuestions.question: A steel casting shows a ragged, branched particle indication at a section change. The most likely discontinuity is a:
options: Cold shut
Hot tear
Lamination
Crater crack
checkQuestions.explanation: Hot tears form when restrained, still-hot metal tears during solidification — they are ragged and branched and favor section changes; a cold shut is smooth and curved by contrast.

checkQuestions.question: Why does MT on the face of rolled plate fail to reliably show laminations?
options: Laminations are nonmagnetic and carry no flux at all
Laminations lie parallel to the surface and to the flux path, so almost no leakage field crosses them at the face
Plate is too thick for any magnetization method
Laminations only exist in castings, not plate
checkQuestions.explanation: Leakage requires flux to cross the discontinuity; a lamination lying parallel to both the surface and the flux disturbs the field very little, which is why it shows at cut edges and bores instead.

checkQuestions.question: On a forging, you find a faint, smooth particle line near the flash line that wipes away easily and is hard to reproduce strongly. The best interpretation is:
options: A false indication — faint lines are never real
A forging lap — its shallow angle and smooth oxide-coated walls leak weakly, so the indication is faint and weakly held
An internal burst breaking the surface
Magnetic writing from another part
checkQuestions.explanation: Laps lie at a shallow angle with smooth, oxidized walls, so the leakage field is weak and the indication faint — a known trap that demands gentle handling and retesting, not dismissal.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about underbead (delayed hydrogen) cracks in welds is correct?
options: They always break the surface at the weld crown
They occur only within the deposited weld metal
They form in the heat-affected zone beneath the bead, may appear hours or days after welding, and favor HWDC because they are subsurface
They can only be detected by radiography
checkQuestions.explanation: Underbead cracking is a delayed hydrogen mechanism in the HAZ; because it is subsurface, half-wave DC with dry powder or a sensitive wet technique is favored, and some codes require a waiting period before final MT.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §5.1 (discontinuities in castings)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §5.2 (discontinuities in ingots)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §5.3 (discontinuities in wrought sections and parts)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §5.4 (discontinuities in welds)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V, Article 7 (two-direction coverage and technique selection — general practice)
